title: 15  Metabolomics Quantification Pipelines
text:
15.13 Summary
This chapter walked through the complete xcms preprocessing pipeline:

Data import via readMsExperiment() — linking raw files to sample metadata.
Peak detection with CentWaveParam — tuning ppm, peakwidth, and snthresh to instrument performance.
RT alignment with ObiwarpParam — correcting run-to-run drift.
Feature grouping with PeakDensityParam — creating a consensus sample × feature matrix.
Gap filling with fillChromPeaks() — recovering low-abundance signals.
Quick QC filter — removing irreproducible features before downstream statistics.

The exported feature matrix is the input for Chapter 17, which covers normalisation, multivariate analysis, and pathway enrichment.

title: 17  Handle Missing Data, Imputation, and Robustness
text:
17.1 Learning Objectives
Missing values are ubiquitous in MS data. Whether a peptide was not detected because its abundance was below the instrument’s limit of detection (MNAR) or because of stochastic sampling (MAR) determines the correct imputation strategy. Using the wrong strategy inflates false positives. This program covers missingness diagnosis, imputation, and sensitivity analysis.
By the end of this program you will be able to:

title: 19  Modeling Covariates and Repeated Measures
text:
19.1 Learning Objectives
A two-group comparison is rarely sufficient. Real MS experiments involve batches, paired samples, time courses, confounders (age, sex, BMI), or repeated measurements from the same subject. Ignoring these structures inflates false positives and reduces power. This program covers covariate-adjusted, paired, and repeated-measures models for MS-based omics data.
By the end of this program you will be able to:

title: 20  Biomarker Modeling Without Data Leakage
text:

title: 21  Pathway and Network Analysis
text:
21.1 Why Integrate Omics Layers?
No single omics layer provides a complete picture of biological systems. Integrating proteomics and metabolomics data — both derived from mass spectrometry — reveals mechanistic connections that neither dataset exposes alone. This chapter covers data structures, correlation-based, and network-based integration strategies using R.

  I --> N[Network Analysis]

title: 22  Build Reproducible MS Reports
text:
22.1 Why Reproducibility Matters in MS Analysis
Reproducibility is a non-negotiable standard for computational mass spectrometry. A published analysis must be rerunnable by others — and by your future self — months or years later. This chapter covers the tools and practices that transform ad-hoc scripts into robust, auditable, and shareable pipelines.

Bioconductor package updates can change results — dependency pinning is essential
Large datasets require pipeline frameworks to avoid manual reruns
Journals increasingly require code and data deposition alongside manuscripts

Meaning

title: 22  Build Reproducible MS Reports
text:
22.4 Quarto for Literate Analysis Reports
Quarto (the system used for this book) is also ideal for individual analysis reports. Embedding code and narrative together ensures that the reported results are always in sync with the code that produced them.

22.4.1 Key report practices
---
title: "LC-MS Metabolomics Report"
author: "Your Name"
date: today
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

## Detected Features

```{r}
cat("Features detected:", nrow(feature_matrix), "\n")
cat("Samples:", ncol(feature_matrix), "\n")
```

## Annotated Metabolites (Level 1–2)

```{r}
annotations |>
  filter(msi_level <= 2) |>
  select(name, formula, mz, rt, score) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3)
```

22.5 Version Control with Git
Every analysis project should be a Git repository. Minimum practice:
# Initialise
git init
echo ".Rproj.user\n.Rhistory\n*.RData\ndata/raw/" >> .gitignore

# Always commit renv.lock
git add renv.lock _targets.R analysis_report.qmd
git commit -m "Initial reproducible workflow"

Exclude

_targets.R
_targets/ store (large, regenerable)


.qmd files
Raw .mzML files (too large)


references.bib
Intermediate result RDS objects

title: 23  Summary and Future Directions
text:
23.2 What We’ve Covered

23.2.1 Part I: Foundations (Chapters 1-2)

MS Principles: Instrumentation, acquisition strategies, and the conceptual foundations of MS data analysis
R Fundamentals: Package installation, core data structures, and the Bioconductor ecosystem



23.2.2 Part II: Data Infrastructure (Chapters 3-5)

Open Data Access: Discovering and reusing public datasets with MsDataHub
Formats and Import: Working with mzML, MGF, and other standards through Spectra
Advanced Infrastructure: Choosing backends and storage strategies for scale



23.2.3 Part III: Spectral Processing (Chapters 6-8)

Preprocessing: Baseline correction, smoothing, normalization, and denoising
Peak Detection: Feature finding, quantification, and signal quality metrics
Spectral Libraries: Reference matching and MS/MS-based annotation workflows



23.2.4 Part IV: Visualization & Statistics (Chapters 9-10)

Visualization: Spectral plots, chromatograms, mirror plots, and quality-control views
Statistical Methods: PCA, clustering, differential analysis, and interpretation



23.2.5 Part V: Metabolomics (Chapters 11-12)

Metabolomics Analysis: xcms workflows, retention-time correction, and feature grouping
Metabolite Annotation: Exact mass search, adduct rules, isotope patterns, and confidence levels



23.2.6 Part VI: Proteomics (Chapters 13-15)

Proteomics Analysis: PSM handling, protein inference, and search-based workflows
Quantitative Proteomics: QFeatures for hierarchical peptide-to-protein analysis
PTM Analysis: Modification-centric identification, localization, and quantification



23.2.7 Part VII: Advanced & Integration (Chapters 16-17)

Multi-omics Integration: Cross-modal analyses with shared experimental designs
Reproducible Workflows: renv, Quarto, pipelines, and publication-ready practices

section: 23.4 Best Practices for MS Data Analysis in R
text:
23.4 Best Practices for MS Data Analysis in R

23.4.1 1. Choose the Right Backend


23.4.2 2. Implement Quality Control

Check coefficient of variation (CV < 30% for technical replicates)
Assess missing value patterns
Monitor batch effects with PCA
Validate feature detection rates



23.4.3 3. Use Appropriate Normalization

Median normalization: General purpose, robust to outliers
TIC normalization: For consistent total signal across samples
Quantile normalization: When distributions should be identical
Internal standards: When available, most accurate



23.4.4 4. Proper Statistical Testing

Use limma for differential analysis (handles small sample sizes)
Apply multiple testing correction (FDR/Benjamini-Hochberg)
Check assumptions (normality, homoscedasticity)
Consider batch effects in design matrix

section: 23.6 Future Directions in MS Data Analysis
text:
23.6 Future Directions in MS Data Analysis

23.6.1 Emerging Technologies

Ion Mobility MS: Additional separation dimension requiring new algorithms
Imaging MS: Spatial metabolomics and proteomics visualization
Top-Down Proteomics: Intact protein analysis without digestion
Data-Independent Acquisition (DIA): Comprehensive MS/MS coverage



23.6.2 Computational Advances

Deep Learning: Neural networks for spectrum prediction and identification
Cloud Computing: Scalable processing of large cohort studies
Real-Time Analysis: Online processing for quality control
Integration: Multi-omics data fusion (proteomics + metabolomics + genomics)



23.6.3 Community Development
The R for Mass Spectrometry initiative continues to evolve:

New backends for emerging data formats
Enhanced visualization capabilities
Improved integration with online databases
Better support for non-standard MS applications

title: 23  Summary and Future Directions
text:
23.8 Final Thoughts
Mass spectrometry data analysis is a rapidly evolving field. The R for Mass Spectrometry ecosystem provides a robust, flexible, and open-source foundation for tackling both routine and cutting-edge analytical challenges.
The skills you’ve developed through this book - from basic data import to advanced statistical analysis - will serve as a strong foundation for your research. Remember:

Start simple: Use built-in functions before implementing custom solutions
Validate thoroughly: Test your analysis pipeline with known standards
Document everything: Future you (and collaborators) will be grateful
Engage with the community: Share code, ask questions, contribute improvements

Thank you for joining this journey through R for Mass Spectrometry. Now, go forth and analyze!


Happy analyzing!
